channel.title: Ahmet Ruçhan Avcı — Blog
channel.description: Blog posts on machine learning, AI systems, and engineering from Ahmet Ruçhan Avcı.

item.title: What I Learned About MCP at the AWS re:Invent Conference
item.description: Key takeaways from AWS re:Invent on the Model Context Protocol — tool calling patterns, security considerations, stateful session design, and real-world integration lessons.
item.content:encoded:
What Is MCP and Why Does It Matter

The Model Context Protocol (MCP) defines a standard interface between LLM inference systems and the tools they call. Think of it as the HTTP of agent-to-tool communication — a wire format and session contract that lets any model, running anywhere, invoke tools defined by anyone, without the two sides needing to know each other's internals.

Tool Calling Patterns in Production

Three architectural patterns dominated the discussion at re:Invent:

Pattern 1: Stateless Tools

The simplest and most composable pattern. Each tool call is self-contained — no session state, no side effects beyond the returned value.

@mcp.tool()
def query_inventory(product_id: str) -> dict:
    """Look up current stock levels for a product."""
    with get_db_connection() as conn:
        row = conn.execute(
            "SELECT quantity, warehouse FROM inventory WHERE product_id = ?",
            (product_id,)
        ).fetchone()
    return {"quantity": row["quantity"], "warehouse": row["warehouse"]}

Pattern 2: Stateful Sessions

Some workflows require accumulating context across multiple tool calls.

const session = await mcp.createSession({
    persist: true,
    ttl: 3600
});
await session.call('init_code_sandbox', { runtime: 'python3.11' });

Security Considerations

Tool exposure introduces an attack surface that most teams underestimate. Key risks include prompt injection via tool results, overly broad tool permissions, tool schema as a vector, and unvalidated inputs.

mcp-inspector validate \
  --schema ./tools/schema.json \
  --strict \
  --output report.json

Key Takeaways

The MCP ecosystem is maturing quickly. The protocol handles the plumbing — the hard work is still in designing tools that are useful, safe, and correctly scoped.
